response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I am going to be honest with you because that is how I operate. At seventy-five years old, working part-time at my salon in Los Angeles, I do not have patience for nonsense, and fake content on social media is exactly that: complete nonsense. I only check social media a few times a month, thank goodness, because every time I log on, I see something that makes my blood pressure rise. People sharing stories that are obviously false, manipulated photos, fake news about everything from politics to health remedies. It is exhausting and frankly infuriating. The way I deal with it is simple and direct. First, I trust my gut instinct. When something seems too outrageous or too perfect, it probably is not real. I came to this country as an immigrant decades ago, and I learned quickly that if something sounds too good to be true, it usually is. That lesson applies to social media just as much as it did to everything else in life. Second, I simply do not engage with content that worries me or seems suspicious. I scroll past it. I do not share it with others, and I certainly do not waste my energy arguing with strangers online about whether something is real or fake. Life is too short for that drama, especially at my age. When I do see my friends from church or clients at the salon sharing things that are clearly false, I will tell them directly and privately. I do not sugarcoat it because I believe people need to hear the truth, even if it makes them uncomfortable. Some appreciate it, others do not, but I sleep fine either way. The real solution, in my view, is that people need to be more skeptical and less eager to believe everything they see on a screen. We managed perfectly fine before social media existed, and honestly, I think we were better off. I spend my free time doing things that actually matter: playing video games, watching good films, knitting. Those activities do not fill my head with lies and anxiety the way social media does.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 75-year-old immigrant hairdresser with low agreeableness and high neuroticism, suggesting direct communication style and tendency toward worry/frustration. Limited social media use (few times monthly) indicates practical, minimal engagement approach. Low openness suggests preference for familiar, proven methods rather than new technological solutions. Moderate conscientiousness means organized but not obsessive approach. Christian beliefs and immigrant background inform trust in traditional values and skepticism. Age and life experience provide perspective that pre-dates social media. Low agreeableness manifests as blunt, unfiltered advice-giving. High neuroticism shows through expressed frustration and anxiety about fake content.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, blunt, slightly irritable, practical, no-nonsense, skeptical, experience-based
